proofs.proof_tex:
Fix \(P\in\mathcal P_\Gamma\), \(\alpha\in[0,1]\), and a realization of \(\mathcal F\). Under \texttt{ass:fisher-sharp-null}, the outcome record \(r\) used by the randomization statistic is unchanged when the assignment is varied. By \texttt{ass:prespecified-score}, every \(s_{ij}(r)\) is therefore fixed under the conditional randomization law. Hence, by \texttt{def:two-tail-events}, \(W=T(Z;r)\) is a well-defined nonnegative random variable on the finite assignment space \(\Omega_n\). Let its distinct conditional support points be \(w_1<\cdots<w_K\), and write
\[
q_k=P\{W=w_k\mid\mathcal F\}>0,\qquad h_k=P\{W\ge w_k\mid\mathcal F\}=\sum_{\ell=k}^K q_\ell.
\]
The conditional product assignment and bounded-odds conditions, \texttt{ass:conditional-product-assignment} and \texttt{ass:rosenbaum-odds}, imply through \texttt{def:rosenbaum-laws} that this conditional law \(P\) is one of the laws over which the supremum in \(p_J\) is taken. Moreover, \texttt{def:two-tail-events} gives
\[
\{W\ge w_k\}=\{T(Z;r)\ge w_k\}=A_J(w_k;r).
\]
Consequently, \texttt{def:robust-two-tail-calibration} yields, for every \(k\),
\[
p_J(w_k;r,\Gamma)
=\sup_{Q\in\mathcal P_\Gamma}Q\{A_J(w_k;r)\mid\mathcal F\}
\ge P\{A_J(w_k;r)\mid\mathcal F\}
=h_k.
\]
It follows pointwise on \(\Omega_n\) that
\[
\{p_J(W;r,\Gamma)\le\alpha\}\subseteq\{h(W)\le\alpha\},
\]
where \(h(w_k)=h_k\). It remains only to verify the survival-transform inequality at atoms. The sequence \(h_1,\ldots,h_K\) is nonincreasing. If no \(k\) satisfies \(h_k\le\alpha\), then \(P\{h(W)\le\alpha\mid\mathcal F\}=0\). Otherwise let \(k_0=\min\{k:h_k\le\alpha\}\). Then
\[
P\{h(W)\le\alpha\mid\mathcal F\}
=P\{W\in\{w_{k_0},\ldots,w_K\}\mid\mathcal F\}
=\sum_{k=k_0}^K q_k
=h_{k_0}
\le\alpha.
\]
Combining the event inclusion with this inequality gives
\[
P\{p_J(T(Z;r);r,\Gamma)\le\alpha\mid\mathcal F\}\le\alpha.
\]
Because the argument holds for every conditional realization of \(\mathcal F\), every \(P\in\mathcal P_\Gamma\), and both boundary values as well as all interior values of \(\alpha\in[0,1]\), the claim follows.
